ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated chocolate biscuit class; the parent class 'biscuit food product' (FOODON_03301710) is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for chocolate biscuit; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chocolate biscuits is approximately 3 pieces (30 g), providing around 138 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chocolate biscuit (record ID F11080009). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: A GI of 40 for chocolate biscuit is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain sweet biscuits and chocolate-coated biscuit analogues (GI range 35–50 for this category). The glycemic load per 30 g serving is calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100 = (40 × 22.83) / 100 ≈ 9.1. This is classified as low GI per the University of Sydney threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). The input record cited GI 40 from an embedded GI database; this value is consistent with the University of Sydney analogous range and is retained with analogous_estimate match type.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (27.9 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g); sodium at 151 mg/100 g is moderate but sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chocolate biscuits due to the high sugar load (27.9 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, even though sodium is moderate at 151 mg/100 g; choose plain wholegrain crackers with less than 5 g sugar per 100 g instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 27.9 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; carbohydrate 76.1 g/100 g with only 1.9 g dietary fibre indicates a highly refined, rapidly digested product.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid chocolate biscuits because the sugar content of 27.9 g/100 g far exceeds safe glycaemic thresholds, and the low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio (1.9 g fibre against 76.1 g carbohydrate) will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if a biscuit is desired, choose oat-based varieties with less than 5 g sugar and more than 3 g fibre per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils, combined with high refined sugar (27.9 g/100 g), raise LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid chocolate biscuits because the combination of estimated saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g from partially hydrogenated vegetable fat), and high refined sugar (27.9 g/100 g) promotes LDL elevation and endothelial inflammation; the American Heart Association recommends limiting trans fat to as close to zero as possible and saturated fat to less than 6% of total daily calories.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g), and high refined sugar (27.9 g/100 g) are all pro-inflammatory and exacerbate RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chocolate biscuits because trans fat (estimated ~0.2 g/100 g from partially hydrogenated oils), saturated fat, and high refined sugar collectively promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; check packaging for 'partially hydrogenated oils' and choose anti-inflammatory snacks such as nuts or oat-based biscuits with no added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate biscuit is a crispy, sweet baked snack product made from refined wheat flour, sugar, cocoa or chocolate coating, vegetable fat, salt, and leavening agents. It is classified as an ultra-processed food and is widely consumed as a snack or light breakfast accompaniment across Singapore and internationally. The texture is characteristically crisp and crumbly, with a sweet chocolate flavour that may come from a chocolate coating, chocolate chips, or cocoa powder incorporated into the dough. Per 100 g, chocolate biscuits provide 459 kcal, 7.6 g protein, 13.8 g fat (of which approximately 6.5 g is saturated fat and an estimated 0.2 g is trans fat from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils), 76.1 g carbohydrate, 27.9 g sugar, 1.9 g dietary fibre, and 151 mg sodium. The high sugar content (27.9 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.9 g/100 g) make this food clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low GI category per University of Sydney thresholds), but the glycemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 9.1, which is non-trivial for diabetic meal planning. Chocolate biscuits are commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast item and are available in numerous commercial formats including sandwich biscuits, chocolate-dipped crackers, and cocoa-flavoured wafers. Allergens typically include gluten and may include milk, soy, and tree nuts depending on the manufacturer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate biscuit?
Is chocolate biscuit high in sugar?
What is the GI of chocolate biscuit?
Can I eat chocolate biscuits if I have high blood pressure?
Are chocolate biscuits suitable for heart patients?
How much sodium is in a chocolate biscuit?
What are the ingredients in chocolate biscuits?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F11080009) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-defined commercial product category with consistent composition across the HPB database. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues for chocolate chip cookies (USDA SR Legacy: Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared) as the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions; this introduces minor uncertainty in fat sub-fractions only. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for sweet biscuits.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific chocolate biscuit entry is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F11080009) as of April 2026; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) was consulted for analogous chocolate chip cookie entries but fat sub-fraction data varies significantly by formulation and cannot be reliably assigned to this generic record. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.2 g/100 g as an estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for commercially prepared chocolate biscuits/cookies, which report trans fat in the range 0.1–0.3 g/100 g from partially hydrogenated vegetable oils; this is an approximation and actual trans fat content depends on the specific manufacturer formulation. 3. saturated_fat_g is set to 6.5 g/100 g as an estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry (Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared, regular). 4. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11080009 and were not available from a single consistent approved source for this generic record. 5. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'wholegrain', which is not supported by the HPB source data for this generic chocolate biscuit record; this tag has been omitted from the output as it is not defensible. 6. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database range for sweet biscuits and chocolate-coated biscuit products (GI 35–50); glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as (40 × 22.83) / 100 = 9.1.
